items.q: What is the difference between the tundra and the taiga biomes?
items.correct: Tundra is treeless cold biome (too cold and permafrost-dominated for trees), while taiga (boreal forest) is the band of coniferous forest just south of the tundra in subarctic latitudes.
items.misconception: Tundra and taiga are the same biome with different names.
items.random_wrong: Tundra is in the Arctic and taiga is in the Antarctic.
items.why: There is no taiga in the Antarctic because there is no continental land at that latitude. The biomes are distinct (treeless vs forested) — defined by whether the growing season permits trees.

items.q: Why are urbanisation rates currently higher in LICs than HICs?
items.correct: HICs urbanised earlier and are now near saturation (80%+ urban); LICs are still in the rapid urbanisation phase, with high rural-urban migration and urban natural increase.
items.misconception: LICs have higher urbanisation rates because they have more cities than HICs.
items.random_wrong: Urbanisation only happens in LICs — HICs are deindustrialising.
items.why: Rate (change) is different from level (proportion urban). HICs have high levels but low rates because most people are already urban. Some HICs even see counter-urbanisation.

items.q: Why does correlation not imply causation in geographical data?
items.correct: Two variables may correlate because of a third confounding factor, coincidence, or reverse causation — statistical association alone does not prove one causes the other.
items.misconception: If two variables correlate strongly, one must cause the other.
items.random_wrong: Correlation and causation are the same thing.
items.why: Classic example: ice cream sales correlate with drownings — both caused by hot weather, neither causes the other. In geography this matters for evaluating claims like 'urbanisation causes obesity'.
The answer examiners want is: Bus Rapid Transit. “Bicycle Rapid Track” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which report defined sustainable development in 1987?
items.why: Correct answer: Brundtland Report. “Stern Review” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximately how many homes are at BedZED?
items.why: Exact answer: 100. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
